sentences.text: Yes.
In 1943, as it was, when I was still in the partisans, I heard that in Warsaw ghetto is a hunger, people dying from hunger.
I didn't know what to do.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My-- my parents lived in one room, in the ghetto, with three other families.
So I talked to my commanding officer.
I said, look, I don't know.
My parents are there.
My brothers are there.
They're dying from hunger.
Maybe I could bring them food.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So what I did?
I got food from farmers.
I-- I tied my sleeves, on my arms, and put in the-- the food, there, in my-- Sleeve?
--sleeves, in my shirt, in my pants.
And I went to Warsaw.
But how you get into the ghetto?
I had a friend, a Polish woman, who lived near the entrance-- one of entrances to the ghetto.
And through her-- she knew somebody at the entrance.
Because the entrance of the ghetto was-- was Polish police, Jewish police, and Germans, at the ghetto entrance.
And somehow.
I got into the ghetto.
This-- this friend of mine lived right nearby the ghetto, on the fifth floor, in an apartment house.
I came to the ghetto.
And I went to my parents' place.
Just horrible, horrible.
People laying in the street, dying.
It's undescribable.
So I brought in the food.
I stayed there one day.
And I-- this little-- this lady helped me to get out of the ghetto.
Everybody. --and your two-- three brothers.
Right.
And I got out of the ghetto.
And she lived nearby, as I said, on the fifth floor.
So before to going, I had to go buy a ticket on the river boat to go back to my partisan group.
But I couldn't do it.
So she had to go and buy a ticket for me.
I mean, they were crying.
Everybody was crying.
My mother told me to stay, not to go.
But I said, no, I'm going to go.
They couldn't.
No.
So anyway.
So, while I was in the ghetto, my-- my brother and I, we used to smuggle meat from the Aryan side.
I forgot about this, too.
You see, the-- there were-- the ghetto was-- had walls, about eight-feet tall walls.
No, all the time in the ghetto.
In the beginning, yeah, we used to smuggle meat.
So I used to stay on one-- on the Aryan side, throw the meat over to the-- to the ghetto, inside.
My brother used to pick it up.
All the [NON-ENGLISH] used to go through the ghetto.
We used to throw-- throw like marmalade.
My brother, we-- we had a place where to-- he stays.
And we used to do it.
That's how we supported the family.
I mean, we needed to do this.
I had fights with the Jewish police.
One time, when I threw over the-- the meat over the-- over the wall-- my brother threw the meat over the wall to me, I got the meat.
And a Jewish policeman came over and want to grab the meat.
I said, no, you're not taking the meat.
--club and starts hitting me.
I grab-- I got the club out of his hand.
And I beat the-- the shit out of him.
So the-- more police came.
They arrested me.
And they took me to a-- the-- where the police was there.
And he--
Yeah.
19-- yeah.
Maybe it was '41.
So I had a fight with him.

So the next thing we knew, after three days being in jail, there, with the police, they took us, about 40 of-- men, who used fight the-- the Jewish police, Ordnungsdienst, to-- to give-- take us to the Germans or the Polish police.
But they took us to the Polish police.
We said, this is the end.
They're going to take us to the Germans.
And this is the end.
So the Polish officer was so nice.
He said, look. you guys beat the hell out of them, of the Jewish police.
But I have to keep you till tomorrow.
Tomorrow, you can go home.
That's the way that it was.
So anyway-- so I went out of the back of the ghetto to this lady.
On the fifth floor, she lived.
This is from the partisan, when you brought them the food?
But after I brought the food to the ghetto.
So when I walked down, I couldn't go with her.
I went down by myself, first.
So I went.
As I was going down the stairs, an SS man and a Polish policeman we're walking up.
And we met during the-- And later on, they came to her house.
They're looking for me.
And she came later on, when she met-- we met in a place.
We met.
She told me this story.
So she came and brought me a ticket.
And I went back to my group.
During the partisans?
The only thing is that we slept on a cemetery.
One winter, I slept on a cemetery in Ukraine.
And I didn't have any-- I had only boots, but only the tops not the bottoms.
The bottom, I had schmattas.
Rags around.
And-- but then, yes.
I had a deal with my mother.
When we-- I had an address of a-- a Polish farmer.
If my mother want to get me, through the partisans, write a letter to this farmer.
In '43, I got this last letter I had and the last thing I knew.
In '43, my mother wrote me a letter, that she and my father and my brothers, she registered everybody to go to work to the east.
You know what this means?
This means Treblinka.
They didn't know that.
I didn't know that.
And this is the last time I heard from my mother.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So after-- after I came, I have to go back when I started about in Danzig.
I was in Danzig.
You heard from your uncle in Paris.
That's where we stopped.
Yeah.
And I got from-- you know, from Paris, everything.
My uncle survived.
He was in Nice, during the war, in the underground.
And--
No.
I met my wife.
Just happened that the commandant of the city of Danzig was a Russian officer, a general, I think.
And they used to give out food for people, who were in concentration camps, every week.
And I-- I went there.
I don't know for what.
And I met Elly on the stairs.
Elly and the two-- and her three girlfriends had a little restaurant in Danzig.
Because a lot of Jewish people were looking for relatives.
They used to go to different cities looking.
They didn't have a-- a place where to eat, where to sleep.
So they knew, this is a restaurant.
You can eat there.
And after knowing Elly about three months, four months, we decided.
Elly didn't know that anybody's alive from her family.
And we got married.
And then Freddy, Elly's brother, Freddy, was looking for her all over Europe.
He heard that she is alive.
And one day, a transport of German, half Jews, came from-- from Danzig to Berlin.
And the head of the-- of the group was the head of the Jewish community in Danzig, who married us.
So my-- Freddy heard that-- that yeah-- that-- Freddy heard that Elly is alive in Danzig.
And she married a Russian officer.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So he says, what?
He didn't know you were Jewish, huh?
So he, somehow-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] right away, he had to go up to Danzig and-- and look for his sister.
Right.
And it took him few weeks to come to Danzig.
He didn't speak Polish.
And I had a gun in my pocket all the time.
And one night-- we used to go out, every night, to a restaurant, Polonia, in Danzig.
That night, we didn't want go away, Elly and I.
So the middle-- at midnight, a knock on the door.
[NON-ENGLISH] Who is it?
Is Elly Diament here?
And she heard the voice.
She starts screaming, my brother.
[CRYING] And [CRYING] I opened the door.
And he came in.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SOBBING] So we stayed there a few, yeah, not long, maybe two weeks.
And
[LAUGHS] Yeah.
Then we decided to leave-- leave Poland.
So we went to Stolp.
Stolp.
We have no papers, no documents, nothing.
Yeah.
So I said, don't worry.
I speak Russian.
I but-- I bought-- took two bottles of vodka in my pocket.
And we stopped a truck in Stolp, a Russian truck, and I-- two officers.
I said, where you going?
He said, to Berlin.
I said, we'd like to go to Berlin, too.
Can we go?
I give you some vodka.
Come on!
So we went near Berlin.
We came to Berlin.
It was raining.
There was no border guards, because it was raining.
So we came up about four miles from Berlin at the railroad station.
And the train was going very, very slow.
And the train hit us, hit our truck.
So they had to fix the truck.
That was the three of you?
You--
Freddy.
Yeah, and two Russians.
So they fixed the truck.
It took a whole day.
We came to Berlin in the Eastern Zone.
I'll never forget, down on Rigaer Strasse was a Jewish committee.
And we registered there.
Then we registered-- Elly comes from the English Zone.
And Elly-- yeah, Elly was pregnant then.
And she was bleeding.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we registered, because we want to go to her town, Gelsenkirchen.
And we-- we went to the-- the Americans took us from the Eastern Zone, in a big truck, to the American Zone.
And we took the train to Gelsenkirchen.
And the city gave us where to stay, with somebody, some German couple.
And from there, we went to Frankfurt am Main.
We went to DP camp Zeilsheim, near Frankfurt am Main.
We-- I worked for the American Army Signal Service Corps.
We used to divide food for the American Army.
From there, I-- we decided we want to go to-- to Israel, to Palestine.
So we went to a kibbutz to learn how to work on the land, to be a farmer.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We stayed there for-- Where was that?
What?
In Germany.
In Fulda, near Fulda.
Near Fulda, where my daughter was born.
Near Fulda, we went to the-- we were in the-- in the, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so-called, farmer's school for about a year.
My daughter was born, in Fulda, in 1946.
Marlene, my older daughter.
Then--
--we-- we-- we tried to go to Palestine illegally.
And I wanted to, because I was a officer in the Polish army.
I wanted to go to Palestine to be an-- serve my life in the army, make a career of the army.
But they just told us that a pregnant woman cannot go on the illegal Aliyah.
At that time, Elly's brother, Saul, who was in Palestine, who was in the Jewish Brigade, wrote us that he wants to come to America to study.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we should go to America.
So we left the kibbutz.
We moved to Stuttgart, in Germany.
We lived there.
And we registered to go to America.
We could have gone, earlier.
Because of my-- of my Polish citizenship, we had to wait for a quota number on my Polish quota.
So we waited-- waited a year and half.
Then, in 1948, January 28th, we arrived in New York.
And where did-- did Freddy go?
Freddy left with the legal Aliyah to Israel.
He was in a kibbutz.
I-- when I arrived in America, I had a lot of families.
My mother had two brothers, which one was alive.
My mother had a sister.
She was deceased.
And I had a lot of cousins.
We were first and second cousins, about 40 of us.
We used to meet.
We had a cousins' club.
We used to meet every three months--
--in New York.
And my-- I had my father's two sisters and my grandmother's-- my grandmother's sister, my grandmother's brother, and my grandmother's nephews.
Yes.
My grandmother, in 1939, left Warsaw to go-- she had two daughters in America-- to come to America after my grandfather passed away in '32.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she was in Paris with my-- with my uncle.
And she was-- when The Germans occupied Paris, she was walking in the street.
And she was taken, in the street, and never heard about her or anything.
So we don't know.
So I worked.
And then I went to-- went to school.
And I worked in the garment industry, in New York, for many years.
When did you arrive, did you say, in America?
I went to school in New York to learn pattern making, designing, chick-- chic school.
And I did-- made a very good living.
Then my uncle-- I had an uncle, my mother's brother-- had a big pocketbook factory in New York.
And he gave me-- told me that, look, I'm getting old.
He's getting old.
He would like to take it easy.
He would like to-- he should go with his sons, be with his sons in the business.
You learn the business, fine.
So I said fine.
So I went to work with my uncle.
I did very well.
Not financially, but very well what-- how-- what I learned.
I learned more in three months than he learned in 40 years.
That he-- I was-- so, after a few months, he let me run the factory.
I had 300 people.
And he went on a-- on a trip, to Argentina, to see his brothers, he didn't see for 40 years.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then I had an out fall with him after five-- four or five years with him.
And I just left him.
And I had a friend in Los Angeles, who was an engineer on the first Apollo project.
He used to work Librascope.
And he begged me to come to Los Angeles.
In-- what year was it, Elly?
We came to Los Angeles in '57, yeah.
I should come to Los Angeles.
So we-- I came to Los Angeles after leaving my uncle.
I was so mad.
I came to Los Angeles.
And I stayed with him, with my friends, for a few weeks.
And Elly had an uncle here, her father's brother, Maury Diament.
It was-- it was nice.
And I got a job in a suit factory, make ladies suits.
The-- the owner was from Germany, a German Jew.
And he liked me, very much, right away.
And I said to him, but I cannot stay here.
My wife is in New York.
I don't have any money.
Don't worry, I give you money.
I give you, just stay here.
Don't go.
You stay here.
Call your wife that you stay here.
That's what I did.
In '50, 1950 in New York.
Alice.
In Jewish Memorial Hospital in New York.
I forgot about her.
[LAUGHS] So this Ernest--
No.
Ernest is the-- the owner of the factory I used to work.
And he liked very much.
I did a very good job with him.
Then he split with his partner.
And he opened a new business.
So he took-- took him with me, I should run his place.
We did very well.
We built up the-- a nice business, again.
Then he-- I ran the place very good.
I worked very hard, very hard.
I give everything.
And he got sick with cancer, some kind of a cancer.
And he said to me, he said, he want me to take-- buy the business.
And Freddy came to California in '58.
Elly's brother, yeah.
So we got together some money and another man, a salesman.
We put together the money.
And we bought this business.
We had the business.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Hannah Strauss.
Yeah.
Very well known firm, very well known garments, everybody knew this.
And we ran the business pretty good, too.
And-- how long were the end of-- 15 years, something like this?
Then I-- when I got-- was 65, I decided to retire.
Oh, me and my-- my older daughter got married.
What year was it?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember.
And then my younger daughter got married.
Then my older daughter got a grandson for me, Aaron.
Then she got another grandson, Daniel.
Then my younger daughter had a baby, Simone.
We just-- unbelievable, unbelievable.
I am very proud of my children and my grandchildren.
And we are a very lucky, lucky, happy people.
Yes.
We are on the-- on the-- I belong to a-- my temple, I when-- the first-- When I came to New York, in '48, I joined a synagogue in '48.
Since then, I've never been without a synagogue.
I belonged to the Temple Beth Hillel, in Valley Village, already 38 years.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was very active in the temple for many years.
We were very active in the City of Hope-- very active.
We're active in the ADL, Anti-Defamation League, for many years.
We're on the original board.
We're also very active in 1939 Club.
This is the Holocaust organization.
Third.
Yeah, and my wife, Elly, is the second time elected to be president of the club.
Third time.
Third time.
Yes, third time.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We are very-- And Mathausen Memorial.
We are very active in the [NON-ENGLISH] organization.
The Council of Postwar--
--Jewish Organization.
Look, we-- we do what-- And I was also-- I'm a founder of the Hebrew University for many years.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Hebrew-- no.
American--
American Friends of the Hebrew University.
Yeah, it's a little hobby.
It used to be a little hobby, but, now, getting harder and harder.
Not really.
I am--
--very lucky.
I have a nice wife, nice children, nice grandchildren.
What else do you want?
You see, my story is very unusual, because a lot of people were partisans much, much later, much, much later.
I started from the very, very, very beginning, one of the first in Poland.
Because they know Korchinski.
Because he was known.
He was known.
In the center of this picture is my great grandfather, Simon Kamm.
Above him is my father, Aron Kamm.
To the left of my father is my mother, Rachel Kamm.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Standing in the back, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] the second man, standing on the left, is my grandfather, David Kamm.
In the second row, sitting next to my great grandfather, is my grandmother, Hannah Kamm.
Underneath, on the bottom, the children are.
On the left side, is me, Ben Kamm.
Next to me, on my right, is [NON-ENGLISH], my brother, younger brother.
The rest of the family is my uncle and aunts.
The rest, cousins.
Approximately, I would say 1931.
In-- in Warsaw.
